methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $58M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Verdant Robotics is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company founded by Gabe Sibley and Curtis Garner that has raised $58M to date. The comparable universe in Robotics & Manufacturing includes one public company (277810.KQ) and eight private peers, among them Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity.

The Stage-Progression method is applicable here, using total capital raised ($58M) as a proxy for current valuation and applying a typical 2.0x step-up multiplier for advancement from Series A to the next funding stage. This approach yields an implied valuation of $116M. The Peer-Median method is not applicable due to insufficient narrow-set comparables; while seven same-sector peers exist, filtering for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier companies yields fewer than three datapoints in any category, making a median calculation unreliable.

The triangulated valuation range derived from applicable methods spans $99M (low) to $133M (high), with a median of $116M. This single-method synthesis reflects meaningful uncertainty: the methodology relies on stage-progression mechanics rather than peer-anchored multiples, and the thin comparable set in this tier and vertical limits cross-validation. Key uncertainties include the appropriateness of the 2.0x multiplier for this specific company's growth trajectory and market conditions, as well as the lack of direct peer data to calibrate against market sentiment in frontier manufacturing robotics.
